analysis.note: Instrumental plural governed by sahita: “accompanied by/with afflictions.”
Formally also Dvitiya Eka; here marked Prathama as predicative adjective of the carried-over neuter subject, with instrumental complements.
Adjectival compound agreeing with kleśaiḥ. Dictionary-level rendering only: “obstructed/restrained and undetermined/unexpounded.”

verification.detail: compound stem nivṛtāvyākṛta as Pum/Trtiya/Bahu: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

samasa.vigraha: ātmadṛṣṭiś cātmamohaś cātmamānaś cātmasnehaś ceti saṃjñitāḥ

analysis.note: Final-member-headed compound in -saṃjñita, agreeing with kleśaiḥ; the first four members are the names of the four afflictions.

verification.detail: compound stem ātmadṛṣṭyātmamohātmamānātmasnehasaṃjñita as Pum/Trtiya/Bahu: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: Grammatically, sahita is an adjective that can take an instrumental complement (“accompanied by/with,” MW s.v. sahita, 1195,1). It cannot agree with the plural kleśaiḥ, so the singular neuter form is best taken with the carried-over neuter subject: “it is accompanied by four afflictions.”
options_considered: sahitam is predicative of an implicit neuter head
justifications.chosen: sahitam is predicative of an implicit neuter head; the instrumental plurals specify what it is accompanied by.

justifications.decision: The compound is instrumental plural masculine, matching kleśaiḥ. As separate words, nivṛta would be morphologically out of agreement; as a compound, nivṛta + avyākṛta yields nivṛtāvyākṛta. Without commentary, its exact technical value remains provisional; the grammar supports “obstructed/restrained and undetermined/unexpounded.”
options_considered: two separate adjectives nivṛta and avyākṛta
a single adjectival compound modifying kleśaiḥ
justifications.chosen: nivṛtāvyākṛtaiḥ is one adjectival compound qualifying the four kleśas.

justifications.decision: The final member saṃjñita means “called, named, termed” when used in compounds (MW s.v. saṃjñita, 1133,3). Thus the preceding fourfold list supplies the names: “called self-view, self-delusion, self-conceit, and self-love.”
options_considered: four separate names governed by saṃjñita
justifications.chosen: ātmadṛṣṭi, ātmamoha, ātmamāna, and ātmasneha are the four names of the afflictions.

justifications.decision: The supplied bhāṣya reading has dṛṣṭi, and dṛṣṭi is lexically “seeing/view” (MW s.v. dṛṣṭi, 492,1). The vulgate ātmaduṣṭi is therefore not adopted for this apparatus.
justifications.chosen: ātmadṛṣṭi is read and translated as “self-view.”

justifications.decision: The dictionary head is saṃjñita (MW s.v. saṃjñita, 1133,3), and the bhāṣya reading supplied for the verse has saṃjñitaiḥ. The variant sañjñitaiḥ is not adopted.
justifications.chosen: saṃjñitaiḥ is analyzed as the participial adjective from saṃjñita, “called/named.”

translation:
It is always accompanied by four afflictions (kleśa),
obstructed–undetermined (nivṛtāvyākṛta):
those called self-view (ātmadṛṣṭi), self-delusion (ātmamoha),
self-conceit (ātmamāna), and self-love (ātmasneha).
one_shot_delta: Without commentary, nivṛtāvyākṛta can only be rendered provisionally from grammar and dictionary; a commentary-blind version may miss the precise Buddhist technical sense often translated “obscured-neutral.”
A commentary-blind translation might fail to carry over the neuter subject and misread sahitaṃ as modifying the four afflictions, although its singular form rules that out.
A commentary-blind version might adopt the vulgate ātmaduṣṭi or normalize sañjñitaiḥ; this apparatus follows the supplied bhāṣya reading but cannot add commentarial confirmation.
analyzer_disagreements: nivṛtāvyākṛtaiḥ: ByT5 segments nivṛta_avyākṛtaiḥ; I analyze the printed form as one inflected compound stem nivṛtāvyākṛta, since a separate nivṛta would not agree with kleśaiḥ in case/number and the form is the standard sandhied compound.

sahitam: ByT5’s compact tag does not safely disambiguate the syncretic form; I mark it as Napuṃsaka Prathama Eka because it functions predicatively with a carried-over neuter head. The same surface could be Dvitiya Eka in isolation.
ātmadṛṣṭyātmamohātmamānātmasnehasaṃjñitaiḥ: ByT5 lists the components; I treat the whole as one inflected compound ending in saṃjñita rather than as separate syntactic words.
open_questions: The exact doctrinal sense of nivṛtāvyākṛta is not settled from the dictionary alone.
The form sahitam is morphologically syncretic between Napuṃsaka Prathama and Dvitiya Eka; the Prathama analysis depends on syntactic carry-over of a neuter head.
The compound label for X-saṃjñita can be described differently in traditional grammar; I have treated it as final-member-headed tatpuruṣa for this checkable analysis.
